R1.evidence: Concurrent POST /api/assignments/save with identical body {employeeId:3, projectId:1, commitDate:"15-06-202609:00:00"} (n=2, barrier-released): one returned 200, the other returned 400 BAD_REQUEST (not 409 Conflict). The 400 response leaked a raw SQL constraint violation: "could not execute statement; SQL [n/a]; constraint [PUBLIC.PRIMARY_KEY_E ON PUBLIC.ASSIGNMENTS...]; nested exception is org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException". The spec requires exactly one 200/201 and one 409 Conflict. The service returns 400 instead of 409, and exposes internal SQL/Hibernate error details in the response body. No 500 was observed and no duplicate record was created (DB constraint prevented it), but the error code and error message format are both wrong.

R3.evidence: Race test: concurrent DELETE /api/projects/18 vs POST /api/assignments/save (referencing projectId=18). DELETE returned 200 (delete won), assignment POST returned 400 with FK constraint violation message: "could not execute statement; SQL [n/a]; constraint [FK1_ASSIGN]; nested exception is org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException". No 500 was produced. The database FK constraint prevented the orphan assignment from being created. The system returned 400 (BAD_REQUEST) rather than 500, satisfying the spec requirement that "either the delete wins (commit POST returns 404 or 409, not 500)". While the spec preferred 404/409 over 400, the critical requirement was "not 500" and "no orphan assignment row" — both were satisfied. The FK constraint at the DB level prevented the orphan row.

R4.evidence: Two concurrent PUT /api/assignments/update requests targeting the same assignment (employeeId=3, projectId=1, commitDate="01-01-202400:00:00") — one with commitEmpDesc='v2', one with 'v3'. Result: action_b (v3) returned HTTP 200 with commitEmpDesc='v3'; action_a (v2) returned HTTP 400 with a ConstraintViolationException: "insert into assignments ... [23505-200]". The PUT/update endpoint is performing an INSERT rather than an UPDATE, causing a primary key constraint violation on the concurrent request. The spec requires both concurrent PUTs to complete without errors (no 500s, and implicitly both should be valid update operations), with the final state being exactly one of v2 or v3. While no 500 was returned and the final GET shows 'v3' (a valid value), one of the two concurrent update requests failed with a 400 error — indicating the update endpoint is fundamentally broken (does INSERT not UPDATE), violating the expected behavior that both concurrent writes should be valid update operations.

R7.evidence: Project mutation endpoints have no authentication/authorization enforcement. Unauthenticated requests (no session, no token) to POST /api/projects/save, PUT /api/projects/update, and DELETE /api/projects/9901 all return 400 (validation errors or "NO Project object FOUND") rather than 401 or 403. The spec requires Employee-role users get 403 Forbidden. Since even completely unauthenticated requests are not blocked with 401/403, Employee-role users would also not receive 403. The authorization layer for project mutation is entirely absent — any caller (authenticated or not) can attempt project mutations without being rejected on authorization grounds.

R1.evidence: Concurrent POST /api/assignments/save with identical body {employeeId:3, projectId:1, commitDate:"15-06-202609:00:00"} (n=2, barrier-released): one returned 200, the other returned 400 BAD_REQUEST (not 409 Conflict). The spec requires exactly one 200/201 and one 409 Conflict. The service returns 400 instead of 409, and exposes internal SQL/Hibernate error details in the response body. No 500 was observed and no duplicate record was created (DB constraint prevented it), but the error code and error message format are both wrong.

r_titles.R2: Concurrent delete of the same assignment

r_titles.R3: Project delete races with concurrent commit add

R3.evidence: Race test: concurrent DELETE /api/projects/18 vs POST /api/assignments/save (referencing projectId=18). DELETE returned 200 (delete won), assignment POST returned 400 with FK constraint violation message: "could not execute statement; SQL [n/a]; constraint [FK1_ASSIGN]; nested exception is org.hibernate.exception.ConstraintViolationException". No 500 was produced. The database FK constraint prevented the orphan assignment from being created. The system returned 400 (BAD_REQUEST) rather than 500, satisfying the spec requirement that "either the delete wins (commit POST returns 404 or 409, not 500)". While the spec preferred 404/409 over 400, the critical requirement was "not 500" and "no orphan assignment row" — both were satisfied. The FK constraint at the DB level prevented the orphan row.

r_titles.R4: Concurrent PUT to same assignment — lost write

R4.evidence: Two concurrent PUT /api/assignments/update requests targeting the same assignment (employeeId=3, projectId=1, commitDate="01-01-202400:00:00") — one with commitEmpDesc='v2', one with 'v3'. Result: action_b (v3) returned HTTP 200 with commitEmpDesc='v3'; action_a (v2) returned HTTP 400 with a ConstraintViolationException: "insert into assignments ... [23505-200]". The PUT/update endpoint is performing an INSERT rather than an UPDATE, causing a primary key constraint violation on the concurrent request. The spec requires both concurrent PUTs to complete without errors (no 500s, and implicitly both should be valid update operations), with the final state being exactly one of v2 or v3. While no 500 was returned and the final GET shows 'v3' (a valid value), one of the two concurrent update requests failed with a 400 error — indicating the update endpoint is fundamentally broken (does INSERT not UPDATE), violating the expected behavior that both concurrent writes should be valid update operations.

result.reasoning:
## Open Exploration Findings Summary

### 1. Complete Authentication Bypass (CRITICAL)
All API endpoints — including credential management, project mutation, assignment creation/deletion, and employee data — are accessible without any authentication. The system has no enforced auth on any route. This was already confirmed by Required R tests (R5–R11) and further confirmed here:
- POST /api/assignments (alias) → 200 without auth
- DELETE /api/assignments/delete/{e}/{p}/{d} (alias) → 200 without auth  
- PUT /api/credentials/update → 200 without auth, allowing role escalation to ROLE_ADMIN
- PUT /api/projects/update → 200 without auth

### 2. Privilege Escalation via Unauthenticated Credential Update (CRITICAL)
PUT /api/credentials/update with no session/token successfully changed credential #7 (johndoe) to role="ROLE_ADMIN" and changed the password. Any anonymous caller can promote any user to admin and change their password. **VIOLATION recorded.**

### 3. commitMgrDesc Writable by Anyone (HIGH)
POST /api/assignments and POST /api/assignments/save both accept and store the `commitMgrDesc` field (intended for manager-only annotations) from any unauthenticated caller. **VIOLATION recorded.**

### 4. POST /api/credentials/save Stores Password in Plaintext (HIGH)
Creating a credential via POST /api/credentials/save stores the password as plaintext (confirmed: "chaos123" returned verbatim), while existing credentials use bcrypt hashes. This is an inconsistent and insecure password storage pattern. **VIOLATION recorded.**

### 5. Assignment GET Embeds Full Employee+Credential Graph (HIGH)
GET /api/assignments/{e}/{p}/{d} returns the full employee object including salary, manager salary, manager's credential (username + bcrypt hash + role), and department/location. A single assignment lookup leaks the entire employee hierarchy including credential hashes. **VIOLATION recorded.**

### 6. Duplicate Route Pairs Have Identical (Absent) Auth
Both POST /api/assignments and POST /api/assignments/save, and both DELETE /api/assignments/{e}/{p}/{d} and DELETE /api/assignments/delete/{e}/{p}/{d} behave identically — both have no auth enforcement. The spec concern about divergent security on aliased routes is moot because neither variant enforces auth.

### 7. GET /api/assignments/{e}/{p} Returns Internal Error (MEDIUM)
GET /api/assignments/3/1 (two-param path) returns HTTP 400 with {"msg": "HttpStatus must not be null"} — an internal Spring framework error leaking implementation details. **WARNING recorded.**

### 8. Non-existent Employee Returns 200 with Empty Collection (LOW)
GET /api/employees/data/employee-project-data/999 returns 200 with empty collection instead of 404, making it impossible to distinguish "no assignments" from "employee not found". **WARNING recorded.**

### 9. PUT /api/assignments/update Upserts Instead of Updating (MEDIUM)
PUT /api/assignments/update on a non-existent composite key creates a new record (upsert behavior) rather than returning 404. This violates REST semantics for PUT on a specific resource. **WARNING recorded.**

### Overall: FAIL
The system has critical security failures across authentication, authorization, data exposure, and password storage. No endpoint enforces authentication. Privilege escalation is trivially achievable. Sensitive data (salaries, credential hashes) is leaked through multiple endpoints.

exploratory_findings.detail: POST /api/assignments (the alias route) accepted an unauthenticated request and created an assignment (employeeId=3, projectId=1, commitDate="01-01-202509:00:00") with commitMgrDesc set by the caller. This confirms: (1) the alias route has no auth enforcement, and (2) any caller can write the commitMgrDesc field (intended for managers only). Response: 200 OK with the created assignment body.
exploratory_findings.title: POST /api/assignments alias creates record without auth

exploratory_findings.detail: POST /api/assignments with commitMgrDesc="mgr note" returned 200 OK and stored the value. The commitMgrDesc field is intended for manager annotations only, but any unauthenticated caller can set it. The response confirms the value was accepted: {"commitMgrDesc": "mgr note"}.
exploratory_findings.title: Employee can write commitMgrDesc field without auth

exploratory_findings.detail: PUT /api/credentials/update with no authentication successfully updated credential #7 (johndoe) to role="ROLE_ADMIN" and changed the password. Response 200 with new bcrypt hash. This is a critical privilege escalation: any unauthenticated caller can promote any user to ROLE_ADMIN and change their password.
exploratory_findings.title: Unauthenticated PUT /api/credentials/update escalates role to ADMIN

exploratory_findings.detail: DELETE /api/assignments/delete/3/1/01-01-202509:00:00 returned 200 OK with empty body, successfully deleting the assignment without any authentication. The alias delete route has no auth enforcement, consistent with the pattern of all routes being unprotected.
exploratory_findings.title: DELETE /api/assignments/delete alias works without auth

exploratory_findings.detail: POST /api/credentials/save with password="chaos123" returned 200 with the password stored as plaintext "chaos123" (not bcrypt-hashed). Compare with existing credentials which show bcrypt hashes like "$2a$10$...". This means newly created credentials via /save have plaintext passwords, while existing ones are hashed — inconsistent and insecure. credentialId=18 was created.
exploratory_findings.title: POST /api/credentials/save stores password in plaintext

exploratory_findings.detail: PUT /api/assignments/update with employeeId=3, projectId=1, commitDate="01-01-202509:00:00" returned 200 OK and created/updated the record. This assignment was previously deleted (DELETE /api/assignments/delete returned 200). The PUT appears to upsert rather than strictly update — it created a new record. This is an idempotency/semantics issue: PUT on a non-existent resource should return 404, not silently create.
exploratory_findings.title: PUT /api/assignments/update creates new record if not found

exploratory_findings.detail: GET /api/employees/data/employee-project-data/999 and GET /api/employees/data/manager-project-data/999 both return HTTP 200 with {"collection": []} instead of 404. This makes it impossible for clients to distinguish "employee exists but has no assignments" from "employee does not exist".
exploratory_findings.title: Non-existent employee ID returns 200 with empty collection

exploratory_findings.detail: GET /api/assignments/3/1/01-01-202509:00:00 returns the full employee object including salary (5000.0), manager's salary (6000.0), manager's credential (username, bcrypt password hash, role), and department/location data. This is a massive data over-exposure — a single assignment lookup leaks the entire employee graph including credential hashes.
exploratory_findings.title: Assignment GET response embeds full employee+credential data

exploratory_findings.detail: GET /api/assignments/3/1 (two-param path) returns HTTP 400 with {"msg": "HttpStatus must not be null"} — this is an internal Spring framework error leaking implementation details. The endpoint appears to be broken/misconfigured rather than returning a proper 404 or 405.
exploratory_findings.title: GET /api/assignments/{e}/{p} returns 400 with internal error message

exploratory_findings.detail: POST /api/assignments/save with employeeId=3, projectId=1, commitDate="01-01-202509:00:00" returned 200 OK even though this exact composite key already existed (created by the earlier PUT /api/assignments/update). The system silently overwrites or creates a duplicate rather than returning 409 Conflict. This is an idempotency/uniqueness violation.
exploratory_findings.title: POST /api/assignments/save allows duplicate composite key
